worth_it_explanation: Nielsen Digital Ad Ratings (DAR) reporting access controls who can view reach, frequency, and on-target percentage data for active campaigns, and who can alter the campaign configuration and demographic targets that determine what DAR measures. An over-permissioned user or a stale account for a departed employee can view confidential campaign performance data or change the audience definition that the entire measurement run depends on. These checks are completed inside the DAR reporting portal's account settings, the remediation is a role downgrade or a user removal, and the resulting access model reflects who actually needs to configure DAR versus who needs to read its output.
intro: Nielsen DAR uses an account-level access model to control who can view measurement reports and who can alter the campaign setup and demographic targets that drive what DAR measures. Users with administrative or configuration access can change the audience parameters, campaign assignments, and account settings that shape the measurement run. Users with reporting access view the reach, frequency, and on-target percentage outputs without altering the settings behind them. These checks confirm that the access levels assigned to users match their actual responsibilities, that accounts belonging to former employees or ended agency engagements have been removed, and that the users who review DAR output are not the same users who can change the parameters that produce it.

audit_checks.title: Users hold broader access than their role requires
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A user with configuration or administrative access in the Nielsen DAR portal can alter demographic targets, campaign assignments, and account settings that govern how measurement is applied. When that level of access is assigned to analysts or agency contacts whose work is limited to reading reports, any of those users can change the parameters behind the data they are reviewing, whether intentionally or not.

educate.base:
Nielsen Digital Ad Ratings (DAR) separates users by what they can do inside the reporting portal. Users with administrative or configuration-level access can change campaign assignments, adjust the demographic target groups that determine what DAR measures as 'on target', modify account settings, and in some account tiers manage other users. Users with reporting-level access can view measurement outputs, reach and frequency curves, on-target percentage breakdowns, and gross rating points (GRPs) without touching any of those configuration settings.

When administrative access is assigned broadly rather than only to users whose job requires it, any of those users can alter a demographic target or a campaign parameter that affects every measurement result tied to that configuration. A change to the target demographic mid-flight, for example shifting the audience definition from adults 25-54 to adults 18-49, changes what DAR counts as an on-target impression for the remainder of that measurement period. A reporting analyst who makes that change while exploring a question about demographic performance alters live measurement data for campaigns they are not responsible for configuring.

The appropriate model is to assign configuration or administrative access only to the users who are responsible for setting up and maintaining DAR measurement parameters, and to assign reporting access to analysts, media planners, and client-side contacts whose job is to read and act on the data rather than change how it is produced.

A few patterns suggest access may be broader than the work requires:
  • Analysts or media buyers who pull DAR reach and frequency reports hold access that also lets them modify demographic targets or campaign configuration
  • Agency reporting contacts hold administrative access because it was assigned during onboarding and was never narrowed to match the work they actually perform
  • Multiple users on the account hold the highest available access level even though only one or two are responsible for DAR setup and configuration
You may want to review the user list in the DAR portal's account settings and compare each person's access level against their actual responsibilities before making changes.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, the analyst reviewing DAR on-target percentage alongside return on ad spend (ROAS) to evaluate media quality is performing a reporting function. You may want to confirm that this analyst does not also hold access that lets them modify the demographic target or campaign parameters the on-target percentage is calculated against.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a client-side contact reviewing DAR reach curves to assess how efficiently the campaign is extending reach against a target demographic typically needs reporting access. You may want to confirm that client contacts reviewing DAR outputs do not hold access that lets them alter the audience definition that determines what DAR counts as reaching the target.

educate.learn_more_note: Nielsen DAR portal user-management documentation is accessible to credentialed account holders through the DAR reporting portal. Contact your Nielsen account representative for guidance on available access levels and role configuration for your account.

investigate.intro: Review the user list in the Nielsen DAR reporting portal's account settings and compare each user's access level against their day-to-day responsibilities.

nielsen_dar.label: Nielsen DAR
steps: Log in to the Nielsen DAR reporting portal and navigate to the account or organization settings. Locate the user management section, which lists each user alongside their assigned access level or role.

For each user with administrative or configuration-level access (the level that allows modifying demographic targets, campaign assignments, or account settings), note their name, email address, and role.

For each administrative or configuration-level user, confirm whether their actual work involves setting up or maintaining DAR measurement parameters. A user whose job is pulling reports, reviewing reach curves, or presenting on-target percentage data to clients is a candidate to move to a reporting-only access level.

Note any administrative users who are agency contacts, third-party vendors, or client-side contacts rather than members of the team directly responsible for DAR configuration. Contacts whose work is limited to reviewing DAR output typically need reporting access, not configuration access.
Document each mismatch: user name, current access level, actual job function, and the access level their responsibilities require.

evaluate.logic: For each user reviewed, record their name, current access level, and whether that level matches their actual responsibilities. A user whose access level matches their job function passes. A user whose access level grants configuration or administrative rights they do not use or need is a fail, recorded with the user's name, current access level, and the access level their work requires.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each user's name, current access level, actual job responsibilities, and whether the access level is appropriate to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Nielsen DAR reporting portal includes users holding administrative or configuration-level access whose day-to-day work is limited to reviewing reports and dashboards. Administrative access in the DAR portal allows modifying demographic targets, campaign assignments, and account settings, any of which can alter how measurement is applied to active campaigns. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the user list against each person's actual responsibilities and moving reporting-only users to the appropriate reporting access level, reserving configuration access for the team members who are responsible for setting up and maintaining Nielsen DAR measurement parameters.

audit_checks.title: Departed staff or former agency contacts still hold active DAR portal access
audit_checks.impact_rationale: The Nielsen DAR portal does not expire or remove user access automatically when an employment relationship ends or an agency engagement closes. A former team member or agency contact who held administrative access retains the ability to view confidential campaign measurement data and, if their account is not removed, to modify the demographic targets and campaign parameters that govern active measurement runs.

educate.base:
Nielsen DAR portal access is managed manually by account administrators. There is no automatic deprovisioning tied to an employment end date, a contract expiration, or a change in agency roster. A user account stays active at whatever access level it was last set to until an administrator explicitly removes or deactivates it.

A former team member who held administrative access in the DAR portal retains the ability to view reach, frequency, and on-target percentage data for active campaigns, and to alter the demographic targets and campaign assignments that determine what DAR measures. A former agency contact who managed DAR configuration retains visibility into measurement data, campaign setup, and reporting that may include sensitive performance information the client has not authorized them to continue viewing. Neither situation requires deliberate misuse to create a problem: a login from an unrevoked account can produce changes that are difficult to attribute after the fact.

Stale access arises in predictable ways. An offboarding process that covers corporate email and VPN access but does not include a DAR portal step leaves the portal access open. An agency transition that removes a contact from internal systems and buying-platform seats does not propagate to third-party measurement platforms unless someone completes that step explicitly. A vendor contact who stopped active work on the account several months ago may still appear in the DAR user list with the same access level held at the start of the engagement.

A few patterns point to stale access in the user list:
  • User accounts associated with email addresses at agencies or vendors the client no longer works with
  • User accounts for email addresses that are no longer active in the client's corporate directory or identity provider
  • Users who appear not to have accessed the portal recently but still hold administrative or configuration-level access
You may want to cross-reference the DAR user list against the client's current roster of active employees and active agency or vendor relationships before removing anyone. A user who appears inactive may still hold a legitimate role.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, a former agency contact who managed DAR measurement for past video or display campaigns may still hold access that lets them view on-target percentage and reach data for campaigns currently in flight. You may want to confirm that access for past agency teams was removed when the engagement ended, not only when the campaign was paused or completed.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a former vendor contact who set up DAR demographic targets for a lead-generation flight may still hold configuration-level access to the current account setup. You may want to confirm that contacts from ended engagements were removed from the DAR portal and not only from the media buying platform.

educate.learn_more_note: Nielsen DAR portal user-management and deprovisioning documentation is accessible to credentialed account holders. Contact your Nielsen account representative for guidance on user offboarding procedures and access review processes for your account.

investigate.intro: Pull the full user list from the Nielsen DAR portal's account settings and cross-reference it against the client's current roster of active employees, agency contacts, and vendor relationships.

nielsen_dar.label: Nielsen DAR
steps: Log in to the Nielsen DAR reporting portal and navigate to the account or organization settings. Open the user management section and note the full list of active users, including each user's name, email address, and access level.

Review each email domain in the user list against the client's current agency roster and vendor relationships. Flag any email domain that belongs to an agency or vendor the client no longer works with.
For internal users, cross-reference the email addresses against the client's current corporate directory or identity provider. Flag any address that is no longer active.
For each flagged user, confirm with the client's account owner whether the person still has a legitimate reason to hold DAR portal access. Remove accounts the client confirms are no longer active.

If the DAR portal displays a last-login date or activity timestamp, note any users who have not accessed the portal recently and who hold administrative or configuration-level access. These are higher-priority candidates for review.

evaluate.logic: For each flagged user, record their name, email domain, access level, and whether the client confirms they are still active. A user confirmed as currently active with a legitimate role passes. A user the client cannot confirm as active, or whose email domain belongs to a former agency or vendor, is a fail, recorded with the user's name, email, access level, and the reason for the flag.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each flagged user's name, email, access level, and active-or-departed status to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} user accounts in {{site_name}}'s Nielsen DAR portal that belong to contacts who are no longer part of the active account team or whose continued association with the account cannot be confirmed. These accounts retain the access level they held when the relationship was active, which in some cases includes administrative or configuration-level access to demographic targets and campaign parameters. {{voice_recommend}} cross-referencing the full DAR user list against the client's current employee and agency roster, removing any account the client confirms is no longer active, and adding a Nielsen DAR access review to the standard offboarding checklist for employees and agency transitions.

audit_checks.title: No separation between users who configure measurement and users who report on it
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When configuration-capable and reporting-only users are not separated in the Nielsen DAR portal, anyone who can view measurement data can also change the parameters behind it. A person reviewing on-target percentage or reach curves can, whether intentionally or not, alter the demographic target or campaign assignment that produced the data they are reviewing. This collapses the separation that would otherwise require a designated owner to approve changes to active measurement configuration.

educate.base:
In a well-structured Nielsen DAR account, the users who configure demographic targets and campaign assignments are not the same users who review the reach and frequency outputs those settings produce. This separation serves a practical purpose: it means that a change to a DAR configuration requires deliberate action by someone who is responsible for DAR setup, and it prevents a reporting-side user from inadvertently altering the measurement parameters behind the data they are analyzing.

DAR settings that can affect measurement outputs include the demographic target definition (which age, gender, or audience segment DAR counts as 'on target'), the campaign assignments that determine which placements DAR is tracking, and any account-level parameters that govern how measurement is applied across the campaign. A change to any of these takes effect on the current measurement run and alters the data that reporting users then see.

When no separation exists and all users hold configuration-level access, several situations become harder to manage. An analyst who modifies a demographic target to explore a hypothesis about what the on-target percentage would look like under a different audience definition changes the live configuration for campaigns they are not responsible for. A client-side contact reviewing reach curves can change the campaign parameters governing that data in the same session. Configuration changes cannot be attributed to a responsible owner because anyone in the account could have made them.

A few signs suggest that configuration and reporting access are not separated:
  • All or most users in the DAR portal hold the same access level, with no distinction between those who configure measurement and those who review its outputs
  • There is no defined group of users who are explicitly limited to read or reporting access in the portal
  • The account has no clear designated owner for DAR configuration decisions, so demographic target changes may have been made by analysts or client contacts during the course of reporting reviews
You may want to identify which users are responsible for DAR configuration decisions and which users consume DAR data in a reporting capacity, then confirm that the access levels in the portal reflect that distinction.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, the analyst reviewing DAR on-target percentage alongside return on ad spend (ROAS) and conversion volume is performing a reporting function. You may want to confirm that this analyst does not also hold access that lets them modify the demographic target the on-target percentage is calculated against. Changing the target audience definition mid-flight would alter the measurement baseline the ROAS analysis draws from.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, the team member who reviews DAR reach curves to assess whether the campaign is extending reach efficiently against a target demographic is performing an analytical function. You may want to confirm that any adjustment to the demographic target or campaign assignment is made by the person responsible for DAR configuration rather than by the analyst who surfaced the question.

educate.learn_more_note: Nielsen DAR portal user-management documentation is accessible to credentialed account holders. Contact your Nielsen account representative for guidance on structuring access levels to separate configuration and reporting functions within your account.

investigate.intro: Review the user list in the Nielsen DAR portal's account settings to determine whether users are differentiated by access level, and whether that differentiation maps to the distinction between configuration responsibilities and reporting responsibilities.

nielsen_dar.label: Nielsen DAR
steps: Log in to the Nielsen DAR reporting portal and navigate to the account or organization settings. Open the user management section and note the access level assigned to each user.

Identify users who are responsible for DAR configuration decisions: setting up demographic targets, assigning campaigns for measurement, and modifying account-level measurement parameters. These users should hold configuration or administrative access.

Identify users whose work is limited to reviewing DAR dashboards, pulling reach and frequency reports, or analyzing on-target percentage data for campaign optimization. These users should hold reporting-only access.
Compare the two groups against the access levels recorded in the portal. Flag any user in the reporting group who holds configuration or administrative access.

If the DAR portal provides a change log or activity history, review it for configuration changes made by users whose role is primarily reporting-oriented. Any such change is a signal that the separation is not functioning as intended.

evaluate.logic: For each user, record whether their access level matches their functional role: configuration or reporting. A user with configuration-level access who is responsible for DAR setup passes. A user with configuration-level access whose function is reporting and who is not responsible for DAR configuration is a fail, recorded with the user's name, current access level, and their actual function. An account where all users hold the same access level with no reporting-only tier is a fail at the account level.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each user's name, access level, and functional role in the account to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Nielsen DAR portal does not separate users by configuration access and reporting access: all or most users hold a level of access that lets them both review measurement data and modify the settings that produce it. This means that anyone reviewing a reach curve or on-target percentage report can also change the demographic target or campaign assignment the report is built on. {{voice_recommend}} identifying which users are responsible for DAR configuration decisions and assigning reporting-only access to all others, so that changes to demographic targets, campaign assignments, and account-level measurement parameters require action by a designated configuration owner rather than being available to any user who logs in to pull a report.
